title: Kinematic Equations Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate kinematic variables instantly: velocity (v=v₀+at), distance (d=v₀t+½at²), and more. Free tool for physics students and professionals.
intro: The Kinematic Equations Calculator solves motion problems under **constant acceleration** using the four core SUVAT equations of classical mechanics. Given any three of the five variables — initial velocity (v₀), final velocity (v), acceleration (a), time (t), and displacement (d) — the calculator derives the remaining unknowns. It applies directly to free-fall under gravity (a = 9.81 m/s²), vehicle braking, projectile launch, and any uniformly accelerated linear motion. This tool is grounded in Newton's laws of motion and is standard in high school and university physics (NIST SI units: m, s, m/s, m/s²).
keyTakeaway: The five SUVAT variables are linked by **v = v₀ + at**, **d = v₀t + ½at²**, **v² = v₀² + 2ad**, and **d = ½(v₀ + v)t** — know any 3, solve the other 2.
useCases: Calculating how far a car travels before stopping given its initial speed and braking deceleration (e.g., v₀ = 27.8 m/s ≈ 100 km/h, a = −8 m/s², stopping distance ≈ 48.2 m)
Determining the final velocity and drop distance of an object in free fall after a set time (e.g., a = 9.81 m/s², t = 3 s → v = 29.43 m/s, d = 44.1 m)
Finding the acceleration of a rocket sled that goes from rest to 300 m/s over 400 m of track (a = v²/2d = 112.5 m/s²)
Solving for launch time of a ball thrown upward at v₀ = 20 m/s to find when it returns to ground level (t = 2v₀/g ≈ 4.08 s)

example.title: Example Calculation
steps: Initial velocity = 0 m/s, acceleration = 9.81 m/s², time = 3 seconds

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The kinematic equations apply **only when acceleration is constant** throughout the interval. The five SUVAT variables are:

| Symbol | Variable | SI Unit |
|--------|----------|---------|
| v₀ | Initial velocity | m/s |
| v | Final velocity | m/s |
| a | Acceleration | m/s² |
| t | Time elapsed | s |
| d | Displacement | m |

The four fundamental equations (any three variables known → solve the other two):

```
(1)  v  = v₀ + a·t
(2)  d  = v₀·t + ½·a·t²
(3)  v² = v₀² + 2·a·d
(4)  d  = ½·(v₀ + v)·t
```

**Which equation to use:**
- Missing **d** → use Eq. 1
- Missing **v** → use Eq. 2
- Missing **t** → use Eq. 3
- Missing **a** → use Eq. 4

---

## Reference Table

Common constant-acceleration scenarios with standard values:

| Scenario | v₀ (m/s) | a (m/s²) | t (s) | v (m/s) | d (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Free fall from rest | 0 | 9.81 | 3 | 29.43 | 44.15 |
| Free fall from rest | 0 | 9.81 | 5 | 49.05 | 122.6 |
| Car braking: 60 km/h → 0 | 16.67 | −8.0 | 2.08 | 0 | 17.36 |
| Car braking: 100 km/h → 0 | 27.78 | −8.0 | 3.47 | 0 | 48.19 |
| Sprinter (0 → 10 m/s in 4 s) | 0 | 2.5 | 4 | 10 | 20 |
| Rocket sled (0 → 300 m/s, 400 m) | 0 | 112.5 | 2.67 | 300 | 400 |
| Ball thrown up at 20 m/s | 20 | −9.81 | 2.04 | 0 | 20.39 |

*g = 9.81 m/s² (standard gravity, per NIST)*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Free Fall (given in the calculator example)
A stone is dropped from rest. After **t = 3 s**, with **a = 9.81 m/s²** and **v₀ = 0**:

```
v = 0 + 9.81 × 3 = 29.43 m/s          [Eq. 1]
d = 0×3 + ½×9.81×3² = 44.145 m        [Eq. 2]
```

Result: the stone reaches **29.4 m/s** and has fallen **44.1 m** — equivalent to a ~14-story building.

### Case 2 — Emergency Braking
A car traveling at **100 km/h (27.78 m/s)** brakes at **−8 m/s²** (dry asphalt, ABS engaged):

```
v² = 27.78² + 2×(−8)×d   →   0 = 771.7 − 16d   →   d = 48.2 m   [Eq. 3]
t  = (0 − 27.78) / (−8) = 3.47 s                                   [Eq. 1]
```

The car needs **48.2 m** to stop — longer than a standard city block intersection gap.

### Case 3 — Upward Projectile
A ball is launched **upward at v₀ = 20 m/s** (a = −9.81 m/s²). Time to reach peak height:

```
0 = 20 + (−9.81)·t   →   t_peak = 20/9.81 = 2.04 s
d_max = 20×2.04 + ½×(−9.81)×2.04² = 20.39 m                       [Eq. 2]
```

Total flight time back to ground level = **4.08 s** (symmetry of constant-acceleration trajectory).

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using g = 10 m/s² instead of 9.81 m/s²** — acceptable for rough estimates but introduces a 1.9% error; always use 9.81 m/s² (NIST standard) in graded problems and engineering contexts.

2. **Ignoring the sign of acceleration** — deceleration must be entered as a **negative value** (e.g., −8 m/s²). Entering 8 m/s² when braking will compute a speeding-up scenario and wildly overestimate stopping distance.

3. **Confusing displacement with distance** — SUVAT equations calculate **displacement** (a vector). If an object reverses direction mid-interval (e.g., a ball thrown up then falling), total *distance traveled* ≠ final displacement.

4. **Applying SUVAT to non-constant acceleration** — these equations are invalid for variable forces (e.g., air drag, spring forces). Use calculus-based integration (∫a dt) or numerical methods instead.

5. **Mixing units** — velocity in km/h, time in minutes, and distance in feet cannot be combined directly. Convert everything to SI (m, s, m/s, m/s²) before plugging into any equation.

---

faq.q: What are the four kinematic equations and when does each apply?
faq.a: The four SUVAT equations are: (1) v = v₀ + at — use when displacement is not needed; (2) d = v₀t + ½at² — use when final velocity is unknown; (3) v² = v₀² + 2ad — use when time is unknown; (4) d = ½(v₀ + v)t — use when acceleration is unknown. All four assume constant acceleration throughout the time interval.

faq.q: What is the standard value of gravitational acceleration (g) used in free-fall problems?
faq.a: Per NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology), standard gravity is defined as exactly **9.80665 m/s²**, commonly rounded to **9.81 m/s²** for physics problems. At sea level this is accurate to within ±0.03 m/s² depending on latitude; the poles (9.832 m/s²) and equator (9.780 m/s²) differ slightly due to Earth's shape and rotation.

faq.q: Can these equations be used for objects moving in two dimensions, like projectiles?
faq.a: Yes — but only by decomposing motion into independent horizontal and vertical components. Horizontally, acceleration = 0 (constant velocity, a = 0), so d_x = v₀_x · t. Vertically, a = −9.81 m/s² (gravity). Each axis is solved separately with SUVAT, then combined for range and trajectory. Air resistance is ignored in this model.

faq.q: Why does the stopping distance increase so dramatically with speed?
faq.a: Because kinetic energy — and therefore stopping distance — scales with the **square** of velocity (from v² = v₀² + 2ad → d = v₀²/2|a|). Doubling speed quadruples stopping distance. At 60 km/h with a = −8 m/s²: d ≈ 17.4 m. At 120 km/h: d ≈ 69.4 m — 4× longer, not 2×. This is why highway speed limits have outsized safety effects.

faq.q: What is the difference between deceleration and negative acceleration?
faq.a: Deceleration simply means the object is slowing down — its speed (magnitude of velocity) is decreasing. In SUVAT, this is represented as **negative acceleration** when motion is in the positive direction. For example, braking at 8 m/s² while moving forward is entered as a = −8 m/s². If you define the positive direction as downward (free fall), then g = +9.81 m/s² and upward throws have a = −9.81 m/s².

faq.q: How do I know which variable is 'missing' to choose the right equation?
faq.a: Identify the variable NOT mentioned or asked about in the problem — that is the missing variable. If the problem gives v₀, a, t and asks for v and d: use Eq. 1 (v = v₀ + at) and Eq. 2 (d = v₀t + ½at²). If time is never given or needed, Eq. 3 (v² = v₀² + 2ad) is ideal. The calculator handles this selection automatically.

faq.q: Are kinematic equations valid in space (zero-gravity environments)?
faq.a: Yes — SUVAT equations are valid wherever acceleration is **constant**, regardless of environment. In microgravity aboard the ISS, an astronaut pushed with constant force still follows d = v₀t + ½at². The difference is that 'g' is effectively zero, so free-fall acceleration doesn't apply. Rocket thrust produces constant acceleration scenarios perfectly suited to SUVAT.

faq.q: What happens if acceleration is zero? Do the equations still work?
faq.a: Absolutely. When a = 0, the equations simplify to uniform motion: v = v₀ (constant velocity) and d = v₀ · t. This is Newton's first law — no net force, no acceleration, constant speed. The SUVAT framework naturally handles this as a special case, making it valid for both accelerated and non-accelerated linear motion.
